narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into building a strong foundation, from your undergraduate days to preparing for the LSAT, and we see the determination in your profile. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.70 stands out well above the typical student's 3.21, and your LSAT score of 36 is close to the average of 36.70, showing solid academic promise. You're planning to attend full-time, just like over 92% of students in our dataset, and your family's middle-income bracket aligns with many others. However, your Year 1 school decile of 1 is much lower than the average of 5.71, and your Year 3 decile of 2 falls below the typical 5.53, reflecting some early challenges in your grades relative to peers. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 40.9%—high enough to tip the decision toward rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants predicted to succeed right away. This prediction was driven primarily by a few key factors in your background. Most notably, your Year 3 decile ranking pushed strongly toward the failure prediction, outweighing even your strong undergraduate GPA, which pulled in the opposite direction toward success. Your LSAT score was the single biggest force favoring passage, providing a significant boost, while your lower Year 1 decile added some concern but had less sway overall. Compared to typical students who pass, your early and mid-year deciles are in the lower range, which carried more weight here than your impressive college performance or family background. While these factors led to this outcome, it's not a reflection of your potential as a Black woman trailblazer in law—many overcome similar hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school deciles, perhaps seeking mentorship tailored to students like you, and reapply with strengthened early academic showings. You're capable of rewriting this story—keep pushing.
